cataloging_subject.label: The Hengwrt MS of Chaucer's Canterbury Tales
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloging unit is Frederick J. Furnivall's edition-level compilation published for the Chaucer Society and represented by University of Michigan item AGZ8233.0001.001. Its principal sequence prints the Canterbury Tales in the order of the incomplete Hengwrt witness, but includes four Wife-Preamble additions from Cambridge Dd.4.24, a Monk-Nun's Priest link restoration from Royal MS 18 C II and Harley MS 1758, two French Melibee comparison quotations from Royal MSS 19 C XI and 19 C VII, and a Parson's Tale ending and Retraction supplied from Christ Church MS 152 and British Library Add MS 5140. Its separately labeled appendix adds the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn, three alternative links, the Canon's Yeoman sequence principally from Lichfield Cathedral MS 29 with two Royal MS 18 C II supplements, and twenty-three teller portraits copied from the Ellesmere manuscript. This record does not collapse that mixed editorial object into the abstract Canterbury Tales, the Hengwrt codex alone, any supplemental witness, the six-part printed carrier, the U-M OCR/TEI encoding, or the checked-in XML artifact.

catalog_summary.date: Canterbury Tales composed c. 1387-1400; title-page range 1868-1879; six parts issued 1869-1881

agents.name: Anonymous author of The Tale of Gamelyn
notes: The appendix and U-M header call Gamelyn spurious in relation to Chaucer's Canterbury Tales; no named author is assigned here.

agents.name: William Harcourt Hooper
notes: The edition credits Hooper with cutting on wood the twenty-three teller portraits copied from the Ellesmere manuscript. The current metadata responsibility vocabulary has no illustrator or wood-engraver role, so that responsibility is stated on the plate content part and in the lineage manifest rather than forced into an inaccurate role.

scope.description: The Canterbury Tales material represented in the principal sequence and the separately supplied Canon's Yeoman sequence

assertion.notes: This does not assign Chaucerian authorship to Gamelyn, Hooper's images, or every alternative appendix link.

scope.description: Assembly of the principal Hengwrt sequence, passage-level supplements, appendix texts, and portrait appendix into this edition-level compilation

assertion.notes: The edition presents itself as a manuscript text edited by Furnivall, but the checked evidence does not identify every person involved in copying, proofing, or production.

date_statements.display: Bound-volume title-page range 1868-1879; issued in six parts from 1869 through 1881
notes: The broad normalized interval preserves both the imprint range and independently cataloged issue dates; it is not the composition date of the medieval texts.

date_statements.display: The Canterbury Tales was begun probably in the late 1380s and remained unfinished at Chaucer's death in 1400
notes: Individual tales may predate the pilgrimage frame, and the edition's witness-specific order is not a securely authorial final arrangement.

date_statements.display: The principal Hengwrt witness is cataloged [1395]-[1405]
notes: This is a manuscript-production range and a qualified first-attestation statement, not a work-composition date.

scope.description: Medieval texts in the principal sequence and textual appendices

Rubrics, explicits, short phrases, and quoted material embedded in the Middle English text

form_statements.description: The edition is predominantly verse but includes the prose Tale of Melibee, Parson's Tale, Retraction, editorial prose, and visual plates.

scope.description: Group B beta combines verse tales and links with the prose Tale of Melibee. Group I combines a verse link with the prose Parson's Tale and Retraction.

The prose passages are the Tale of Melibee within mixed Group B beta, the Parson's Tale and Retraction within mixed Group I, and the editorial frame. This selected-passages scope does not classify either whole mixed group as prose.
A constituent mode in several tales, serious and parodic; not the genre of every component.
A constituent comic genre in the Miller, Reeve, Cook, Summoner, and related tale traditions.

subject_statements.label: Religion, devotion, anticlerical satire, and penitence

summaries.value: Furnivall's Chaucer Society edition presents the Canterbury Tales in the distinctive order of the incomplete Hengwrt manuscript: A-D-H-B alpha-F-G-E-C-B beta-I. It preserves twenty-three conventional tales in that principal stream, repairs the lost ending of the Parson's Tale and Retraction from two other manuscripts, and supplies the absent Canon's Yeoman sequence from Lichfield in an appendix. The same appendix also prints the non-Chaucerian Tale of Gamelyn, three alternative links, and twenty-three Ellesmere-derived teller portraits. The resulting object is a manuscript-centered but explicitly multi-source scholarly compilation, not a homogeneous transcript of Hengwrt.

The XML has ten principal group divisions and forty-seven main DIV2 units, followed by seven textual appendix divisions. The intellectual content comprises the General Prologue, twenty-four conventional Canterbury tales when the Lichfield Canon's Yeoman supply is counted, their prologues and links, the Retraction, the collateral Tale of Gamelyn, three separately sourced links, and a plate appendix. The announced Cambridge Gg.4.27 figure set is absent from the inspected complete scan and from U-M's encoded content.

The record follows the exact edition-level boundary encoded by agz8233. Manuscript production, physical order, page-image access, modern OCR, XML markup, and rights are held in the linked lineage record. The complete cached edition scan is a differently bound copy: Gamelyn follows printed page 128 there even though the intellectual appendix list and U-M serialization place it after the main Hengwrt text.

extent.description: Twenty-four conventional Canterbury tales are represented: twenty-three in the principal Hengwrt-order stream and the Canon's Yeoman Tale supplied from Lichfield in the appendix. Gamelyn is collateral and contributes zero to that count. The XML has 47 main DIV2 units and seven textual appendix DIV1 units, for 54 retained textual encoding units. Excluding note descendants, BODY contains 16,405 L elements and BACK contains 1,906, for 18,311 line elements; 902 belong to Gamelyn, leaving 17,409 in Chaucerian main-and-appendix material. The XML also has 614 LG elements, 217 P elements, and 804 PB milestones. These are encoding representation metrics, not a new critical lineation or a claim that prose serialized in P is verse.

content_parts.label: Canterbury Tales in Hengwrt order
content_parts.summary: The principal A-D-H-B alpha-F-G-E-C-B beta-I sequence, containing the General Prologue and twenty-three conventional tales. Hengwrt lacks the Canon's Yeoman sequence and loses the Parson's Tale after X.1180; the edition's latter gap alternates between surviving runs in Christ Church MS 152 and two discontiguous supplies from Add MS 5140.

locators.value: original parchment ff. 2r-250v, with X.1180-end absent

content_parts.summary: General Prologue; Knight, Miller, Reeve, and incomplete Cook sequence.

content_parts.label: Group D, Fragment V
content_parts.summary: Wife of Bath, Friar, Summoner, Monk, and Nun's Priest sequence.

notes: The XML serializes Melibee primarily in P elements; it is not counted as verse because adjacent units use L.

content_parts.label: Group I, Fragment X
content_parts.summary: Parson link, an omitted editorial analysis, the prose Parson's Tale, and Chaucer's Retraction. After Hengwrt breaks at X.1180, the edition uses Christ Church MS 152 milestones ff. 262r-271v, changes to Add MS 5140 ff. 348r-351r for [887]-[944] because Christ Church has lost ff. 272-273, returns to Christ Church ff. 274r-276v through [1044], and then changes to Add MS 5140 ff. 355v-357v for the ending and Retraction.

locators.value: two represented runs after Hengwrt: manuscript milestones ff. 262r-271v and ff. 274r-276v; interrupted by Add MS 5140 at [887]-[944] and ending after [1044]
ff. 262r-271v at canvases 523-542 and ff. 274r-276v at canvases 543-548 of the complete 684-canvas presentation
two discontiguous supplies: ff. 348r-351r for [887]-[944], then ff. 355v-357v after [1044] through the final passage and Retraction

notes: The 74 L elements belong to the verse link; the tale and Retraction are prose represented in P elements.

content_parts.summary: A twenty-eight-line alternative link printed from British Library Royal MS 18 C II.

content_parts.summary: A thirty-two-line alternative link printed from British Library Harley MS 7333, f. 68v, column 2.

content_parts.label: Canon's Yeoman sequence
content_parts.summary: The Second Nun-Canon's Yeoman link, Canon's Yeoman preamble, and Canon's Yeoman Tale, supplied principally from Lichfield Cathedral MS 29 ff. 172v-183v because Hengwrt lacks G.554-1481, with short Royal MS 18 C II supplements at G.717 and G.1337-1340.

locators.value: ff. 172v-183v, excluding Royal MS 18 C II supplements G.717 and G.1337-1340

content_parts.label: Drawings of the twenty-three tellers
content_parts.summary: Twenty-three teller portraits copied from the Ellesmere manuscript and cut on wood by William Harcourt Hooper. The edition allowed them to be bound near their tales or together because they were not considered part of the text.

related_works.title: The Canterbury Tales
notes: The edition contains a witness-ordered and partly supplied representation of the Canterbury Tales, not a single homogeneous Hengwrt transcription and not an authorially finalized fragment order.

related_works.title: The Tale of Gamelyn
notes: The edition explicitly labels Gamelyn spurious and prints it as appendix matter; it is not counted among Chaucer's twenty-four conventional tales.

evidence.summary: Verifies ten principal groups, forty-seven main DIV2 units, seven textual appendix divisions, exact group/page ranges, 18,311 non-note BODY/BACK L elements, 614 LG, 217 P, 804 unique sequential PB references, and every non-generic manuscript milestone: Dd.4.24 Wife additions; Royal/Harley Monk-Nun supplies; Royal 19 French source quotations; Christ Church/Add MS 5140 Parson transitions; and Royal 18 C II/Lichfield Canon transitions, alongside the appendix witnesses.

open_questions.question: How should each separately issued Chaucer Society part, its printed date, later title-page range, and bound-copy arrangement be crosswalked bibliographically?
next_steps: Collate complete copies and Chaucer Society issue lists to map each of First Series nos. 3, 9, 27, 39, 51, and 71 to its exact issue date and contents.
Preserve intellectual appendix order separately from each bound copy's physical placement of movable matter.

open_questions.question: Were the announced six teller and six allegorical figures from Cambridge University Library MS Gg.4.27 ever issued for this Hengwrt edition?
next_steps: Inspect additional bound copies and Chaucer Society archival records.
Do not count or model the Gg.4.27 figures as extant edition content unless an issued copy is found.
notes: The complete inspected scan lacks them, and U-M reports them wanting and perhaps never issued.

open_questions.question: What current scholarship concludes about the authenticity, intended placement, and relation of each alternative link printed in the appendix?
next_steps: Consult a current critical edition and manuscript-specific scholarship before assigning every appendix link to Chaucer or integrating it into one canonical order.
Retain the edition's witness-specific labels and separate part boundaries meanwhile.

open_questions.question: What composition date, dialect region, and preferred current genre description should be assigned specifically to the appendix's Tale of Gamelyn?
next_steps: Consult a current critical edition or dedicated scholarly record for Gamelyn rather than transferring dates or dialect from Harley 1758, Royal 17 D XV, or Christ Church MS 152.

open_questions.question: How should the edition's printed lineation, Six-Text cross-references, raw XML L/LG structure, prose serialization, and modern canonical Canterbury Tales numbering be crosswalked?
next_steps: Retain the current reproducible representation metrics without presenting them as a new critical lineation.
Build a passage-level concordance against a current critical edition before normalizing line identifiers.

notes: The group sequence A-D-H-B alpha-F-G-E-C-B beta-I is Furnivall's manuscript-centered order for this edition; it is not silently generalized as Chaucer's final authorial ordering.
Hengwrt remains the principal source even though Group I becomes passage-level composite and the Canon's Yeoman sequence is supplied in the appendix.
Gamelyn is an appendix work, not a twenty-fifth Chaucerian Canterbury tale. Its 902 L elements are included in the edition-level representation total but excluded from the twenty-four-tale count.
The complete edition scan and the U-M/XML serialization arrange separately issued appendix matter differently. Intellectual/editorial sequence and carrier binding order are both preserved.

The two brief Royal 19 French snippets are apparatus/source quotations inside Melibee, not a substantive work-level language facet; their language and function are retained only in scoped locators and lineage relations.
The twenty-three Ellesmere portraits are visual edition content and do not turn this scan into a complete manuscript facsimile of Ellesmere.
Rights, public access, cache presence, request routes, and provider terms are recorded per entity in the linked lineage manifest.
